request.question: What number did the song "Reapers"  by Muse reach on Switzerland's main music sales chart?

title: Swiss Hitparade
List of Gaon Digital Chart number ones of 2017
Regional Mexican Albums
Reapers (song)
Canadian Albums Chart
List of Gaon Digital Chart number ones of 2010
Shut Me Up
Evening Wear/Mark David Chapman
The Christmas Album (Neil Diamond album)
Bardeux
sentences: The Swiss Hitparade (German: Schweizer Hitparade ) are Switzerland's main music sales charts.
The charts are a record of the highest-selling singles and albums in various genres in Switzerland.
The Gaon Digital Chart is a chart that ranks the best-performing singles in South Korea.
Managed by the domestic Ministry of Culture, Sports and Tourism (MCST), its data is compiled by the Korea Music Content Industry Association and published by the Gaon Music Chart.
The ranking is based collectively on each single's download sales, stream count, and background music use.
In mid-2008, the Recording Industry Association of Korea ceased publishing music sales data.
The MCST established a process to collect music sales in 2009, and began publishing its data with the introduction of the Gaon Music Chart the following February.
With the creation of the Gaon Digital Chart, digital data for individual songs was provided in the country for the first time.
Gaon provides weekly (listed from Sunday to Saturday), monthly and yearly charts.
Below is a list of singles that topped the weekly and monthly charts.
The Regional Mexican Albums is a genre-specific record chart published weekly by "Billboard" magazine in the United States.

The chart was established in June 1985 and originally listed the top twenty-five best-selling albums of mariachi, tejano, norteño, banda and duranguense, which are frequently considered regional Mexican.

The genre is considered by musicologist as being "the biggest-selling Latin music genre in the United States", and represented the fastest ever growing Latin genre in the United States after tejano music entered the mainstream market during its 1990s golden age.
Originally, "Billboard" based their methodology on sales surveys it sent out to record stores across the United States and by 1991 began monitoring point-of-sales compiled from Nielsen Soundscan.

Musicologist and critics have since criticized the sales data compiled from Nielsen, finding that the company only provides sales from larger music chains than from small shops that specialized in Latin music—where the majority of Latin music sales are generated.
The magazine decided to rank Latin music recordings in August 1970 under the title Hot Latin LPs, which only ranked the best-selling Latin albums in Los Angeles (Pop) and the East Coast (Salsa).
Before the chart's inception, musicians' only chart success was the Texas Latin LPs (formerly the San Antonio Latin LPs) section where regional Mexican music was more prominent.
Beginning in November 1993, "Billboard" lowered the rankings from twenty-five to fifteen positions on its Latin genre-specific charts, while the Top Latin Albums expanded to fifty titles.
From July 2001 until April 2005, the chart increased to twenty titles and then lowered back to fifteen titles.
Since 2009, the Regional Mexican Albums chart list the top twenty best-selling albums determined by sales data compiled from Neilsen Soundscan.
"Reapers" is a song by English rock band Muse.

It was released as the second promotional single from the band's seventh studio album "Drones", and was given a 7" single release, as part of Record Store Day 2016, on 16 April 2016 as the fifth and final single from Drones.

It peaked at number 75 on the French Singles Chart, 71 on the Swiss Hitparade singles chart, 37 on "Billboard"' s Hot Rock Songs, and became Muse's highest-charting single on "Billboard"' s Mainstream Rock Songs at number 2.
The Canadian Albums Chart is the official album sales chart in Canada.
It is compiled every Monday by U.S.-based music sales tracking company Nielsen SoundScan, and published every Tuesday by "Billboard".
The Gaon Digital Chart is a chart that ranks the best-performing singles in South Korea.
Managed by the domestic Ministry of Culture, Sports and Tourism (MCST), its data is compiled by the Korea Music Content Industry Association and published by the Gaon Music Chart.
The ranking is based collectively on each single's weekly download sales, stream count, and background music usage.
In mid-2008, the Recording Industry Association of Korea ceased publishing music sales data.
The MCST established a process to collect music sales in 2009, and began publicly publishing its data with the introduction of the Gaon Music Chart the following February.
With the creation of the Gaon Digital Chart, digital data for individual songs was provided in the country for the first time.
"Shut Me Up" is a single by Mindless Self Indulgence, released on September 12, 2006.
It is the first track on their third studio album, "You'll Rebel to Anything".
"Shut Me Up" peaked at number 7 on the "Billboard" Hot Singles Sales chart and number 1 on the Hot Dance Singles Sales chart.
"Evening Wear" and "Mark David Chapman" are songs by Mindless Self Indulgence, released as a double A-side single on January 19, 2009 in the UK and January 20, 2009 in North America.
This single includes the video for "Mark David Chapman" in four different formats, directed by Mike Dahlquist, and the previously unreleased song, "Written in Cold Blood".
"Evening Wear/Mark David Chapman" peaked at number 2 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 Singles Sales chart and number 1 on the Hot Dance Singles Sales chart.
The Christmas Album is the twentieth studio album by Neil Diamond and his first to feature Christmas music.
It features orchestral and choir arrangements by David Campbell.
The album reached No. 8 on the "Billboard" 200 album sales chart, No. 50 on the UK album sales chart, and No. 30 on Australian music chart.
Bardeux is a dance duo from the 1980s.
The group was formed in 1986 and consisted of Stacy "Acacia" Smith and Tairrie B.
The group's first single "Three Time Lover", went to #10 on Billboard's Hot Dance Singles Sales chart.
Tairrie B left the group to pursue a solo career and was replaced by Lisa "Jaz" Teaney.
In 1988 the group released their album "Bold as Love" and their second single "Magic Carpet Ride".
The single went to #5 on Billboard's Hot Dance Singles Sales chart and #81 on the "Billboard" Hot 100.
The group performed the song on Club MTV.
"When We Kiss", the third single from the album, would turn out to be the group's biggest hit going all the way to #36 on the Billboard Hot 100.
The fourth single released from the album, "Bleeding Heart", was a top 40 hit on the Billboard's Hot Dance Singles Sales chart.
A fifth and last single release from that album, "Hold Me, Hold Me" did not chart.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: What number did the song "Reapers"  by Muse reach on Switzerland's main music sales chart?
hop1_docs: Reapers (song) | "Reapers" is a song by English rock band Muse.  It was released as the second promotional single from the band's seventh studio album "Drones", and was given a 7" single release, as part of Record Store Day 2016, on 16 April 2016 as the fifth and final single from Drones.  It peaked at number 75 on the French Singles Chart, 71 on the Swiss Hitparade singles chart, 37 on "Billboard"' s Hot Rock Songs, and became Muse's highest-charting single on "Billboard"' s Mainstream Rock Songs at number 2.

What I Did Right | "What I Did Right" is a song recorded by American country music group Sons of the Desert.  It was released in February 2001 as the third single from the album "Change".  The song reached number 22 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks chart.  Lead singer Drew Womack wrote the song with Sonny LeMaire.

Oh What It Did to Me | "Oh What It Did to Me" is a song written by Jerry Crutchfield, and recorded by American country music artist Tanya Tucker.  It was released in February 1991 as the fourth single from her album "Tennessee Woman".  The song reached number 12 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks chart in June 1991.

Muse (Jolin Tsai album) | Muse is the twelfth studio album by Taiwanese singer Jolin Tsai ( ).  It was released on September 12, 2012, by Warner Music Taiwan.  The artistically-flavored album actually brought back all the sales that were lost in her last studio album, "Myself" (2010).  Referred to as a "pop masterpiece", the album houses many tracks that are influenced by the elements of art, poetry, and dance.  Critical and commercial response to the album was positive, it has sold more than 100,000 copies sold in Taiwan alone, and made her the best-selling female singer of the year in Taiwan.  The lead single, "The Great Artist", reached number 2 on the Hit FM Top 100 Singles of the Year.  The second track, "Dr. Jolin", reached number 10 on the chart.  The eighth track, "Wandering Poet", reached number 24 on the chart.  The album earned Tsai four Golden Melody Award nominations for Best Mandarin Album, Best Mandarin Female Singer, Best Music Video, and Song of the Year, and she finally won for Song of the Year for the lead single, "The Great Artist".  The album also earned her an MTV Europe Music Award nomination for Best Asian Act.

Hysteria (Muse song) | "Hysteria" (also known as "Hysteria (I Want It Now)" in the United States) is a song by English alternative rock band Muse and is featured on their third studio album, "Absolution".  It was also released as a single from that album on 1 December 2003 in the United Kingdom, peaking at number 17 in the UK Singles Chart (see 2003 in British music).  The song is also well known for its intricate bass line, which was voted the sixth best bass line of all time on MusicRadar.  It reached number 9 in the US on the "Billboard" Modern Rock Tracks chart.

A Song of Joy | "A Song of Joy" ("Himno de la alegría") is the title of a popular rock song by the Spanish singer and actor Miguel Ríos.  The song is set to the tune the Ninth Symphony by Ludwig van Beethoven, as arranged by Waldo de los Rios, who specialized in arranging classical music to contemporary rhythms.  The single was enormously popular in many countries in 1970 (see 1970 in music), reaching number one on music charts in Australia, Canada, Germany, Switzerland, and the Easy Listening chart in the United States.  On the U.S. pop chart, the song peaked at number 14 and was the only Top 40 hit for Ríos.  In the United Kingdom, the song reached number 16 on the British pop chart.  In Germany, the song is the most successful pop hymn ever.

Too Much (Elvis Presley song) | "Too Much" is a #1 song recorded in a hit version by Elvis Presley and published by Elvis Presley Music in 1956.  It was written by Bernard Weinman and Lee Rosenberg.  It was first recorded in 1954 by Bernard Hardison on Republic Records.  Elvis Presley recorded the song in September 1956 and first performed it on January 6, 1957 on CBS-TV's "The Ed Sullivan Show."  Released as a single, Presley's "Too Much" reached number one on both the Cashbox and Billboard sales charts and went to number three on the R&B chart.  The single peaked at number two on the then-named Top 100 chart, the main Billboard pop chart.
hop1_titles: Oh What It Did to Me
response.summary_1: First-hop evidence: Passage [1] says the song "peaked ... 71 on the Swiss Hitparade singles chart." Bridge entity: Reapers (song). Missing fact: the song's peak position on Switzerland's main sales chart is required — the passages report it as 71 (so the fact is present). Retrieval directions: 1) Open the "Reapers (song)" article (or its chart table) to confirm the Swiss Hitparade peak and view the citation; 2) if needed, check the Swiss Hitparade archive (site:hitparade.ch) or search '"Reapers" Muse site:hitparade.ch' to verify.
hop2_docs: Reapers (song) | "Reapers" is a song by English rock band Muse.  It was released as the second promotional single from the band's seventh studio album "Drones", and was given a 7" single release, as part of Record Store Day 2016, on 16 April 2016 as the fifth and final single from Drones.  It peaked at number 75 on the French Singles Chart, 71 on the Swiss Hitparade singles chart, 37 on "Billboard"' s Hot Rock Songs, and became Muse's highest-charting single on "Billboard"' s Mainstream Rock Songs at number 2.

Valley of the Muses | The Valley of the Muses was the site of an ancient Greek sanctuary to the Muses and the "Mouseia" festivals held in their honor.  It is an open-air historical site open permanently to the public.  It is located at Thespies on the eastern slopes of Mount Helicon in Boeotia, Greece.

Best of Grim Reaper | Best of Grim Reaper is a compilation album by Grim Reaper.  The songs are drawn from Grim Reapers singles and albums, including the three albums which made the Billboard 200 Albums Chart.

Wikidumper.org | WikiDumper.org is a website created by Clifford A. Pickover that promises to permanently record a snapshot of the "best of the Wikipedia rejects", articles that are slated for deletion at the site.  WikiDumper was launched on November 4, 2006, and accepts user submissions.  Although the site doesn't specify its criteria for inclusion, many of its articles don't cite their sources.  The site has been criticized as likely to be less accurate than Wikipedia.

Reaper (song) | "Reaper" is a song from Australian singer and songwriter Sia's seventh studio album "This Is Acting" (2016).  The song was co-written by Sia, Kanye West, Noah Goldstein, Charles Misodi Njapa (better known by his stage name 88-Keys), and Dom $olo, originally for Rihanna's album "Anti" (2016).  "Reaper" was co-produced by West, Dom $olo, Goldstein, 88-Keys, Jesse Shatkin, and Jake Sinclair.  Sia and West had previously collaborated on the latter's song "Wolves".
Boeotian muses | The three original, Boeotian muses were Mneme (the muse of memory), Melete (the muse of meditation) and Aoede (the muse of song).

Adobe Muse | Adobe Muse is a website builder that allows designers to create fixed, fluid, and adaptive websites without having to write any code.  Muse generates static websites giving users the freedom to host their sites with any hosting provider.  Users can add more advanced functionality such as blogging and eCommerce to their website with plugins created by third-party developers.  This application is available through Adobe's Creative Cloud subscription.
response.summary_2: "Reapers" by Muse reached number 71 on Switzerland's main music sales chart, the Swiss Hitparade; passage [1] reports it peaked at number 71 on the Swiss Hitparade, confirming the context.
